questions.prompt: User has Reduce Transparency enabled. Your app uses materials. What happens?

choices.text: Standard SwiftUI / UIKit materials respect the setting automatically — they render with reduced or no transparency, falling back to a solid background appropriate for the mode. Users with Reduce Transparency enabled (vision impaired, motion-sensitive, attention-focused) see solid surfaces instead of blurred ones; readability improves; the visual hierarchy still works because system uses appropriate solid tones for each material level. If you use RAW blur effects (UIVisualEffectView with custom config), you must check `UIAccessibility.isReduceTransparencyEnabled` and provide a solid fallback. In SwiftUI: `@Environment(\\.accessibilityReduceTransparency)` lets you adapt. The setting exists because translucent surfaces can be hard to read (especially for low-vision users) and visually distracting (for users sensitive to motion / busy backgrounds). Honoring it is mandatory for accessibility-conscious apps; Apple's App Store review increasingly checks. Free if you use system materials; one check if you customize.

questions.explanation: System materials auto-adapt; custom blur needs `isReduceTransparencyEnabled` check + solid fallback.

questions.prompt: List view has translucent material backgrounds on every cell. Scrolling stutters on older devices. Cause?

choices.text: GPU cost of compositing many materials simultaneously. Each translucent surface requires the GPU to sample what's behind, blur it, blend with the material's tint — cheap for one toolbar, expensive when 50 list cells each have material backgrounds rendering at 60fps. Fix: reserve materials for floating chrome (toolbars, sheets, the occasional card on rich content); use solid backgrounds for cells in lists / grids. Profile on the oldest device you support (older A11/A12 chips hit material cost hardest); Instruments → Core Animation shows offscreen rendering. The visual difference is real but the performance cost on lists outweighs the aesthetic gain. Alternative: tinted solid backgrounds (`secondarySystemGroupedBackground`) achieve hierarchy without the GPU cost. Apple's own Mail / Messages / Notes use solid backgrounds for list cells; materials reserved for the navigation/toolbar layers. Materials are a tool, not a default — use deliberately.

questions.explanation: GPU compositing cost of many materials. Reserve materials for chrome; use solid backgrounds for list cells.
